snippets.title: Sigmoid and derivative from scratch
snippets.explanation: Shows saturation: at |x|=10 the output is ~0 or ~1 and the derivative is ~0; at x=0 the derivative peaks at 0.25.

snippets.title: Numerically stable sigmoid
snippets.explanation: Branches avoid exp(large positive) overflow. Production code and frameworks use equivalent stable forms.

snippets.title: Vanishing gradient through stacked sigmoid layers
snippets.code:
import numpy as np

def sigmoid(x):
    return 1.0 / (1.0 + np.exp(-np.clip(x, -500, 500)))

# Ten hidden layers, each with pre-activation x=4 (saturated)
x = 4.0
product = 1.0
for layer in range(10):
    s = sigmoid(x)
    ds = s * (1.0 - s)
    product *= ds
    print(f"layer {layer+1}: sigma'={ds:.6f}, cumulative product={product:.2e}")
snippets.explanation: With x=4 each layer contributes σ′≈0.018. Multiplying across 10 saturated sigmoid layers shrinks the backprop signal dramatically — why deep hidden stacks avoid sigmoid.

snippets.title: PyTorch binary head: Linear + sigmoid at inference
snippets.code:
import torch
import torch.nn as nn

model = nn.Sequential(
    nn.Linear(8, 16),
    nn.ReLU(),
    nn.Linear(16, 1),
)
loss_fn = nn.BCEWithLogitsLoss()  # sigmoid fused inside loss

X = torch.randn(4, 8)
y = torch.tensor([[1.0], [0.0], [1.0], [0.0]])

logits = model(X)
loss = loss_fn(logits, y)
loss.backward()

with torch.no_grad():
    probs = torch.sigmoid(logits)
print("probs:", probs.squeeze().tolist())
snippets.explanation: ReLU hidden layers avoid vanishing gradients; sigmoid appears only on the output probability via torch.sigmoid at inference, while training uses logits with BCEWithLogitsLoss.
